llm_response.root_cause: The instance ran out of memory due to the large model size and high memory usage of the python3 process, triggering the Linux OOM killer.
llm_response.timeline_summary: The instance started loading a large model (bert-large-uncased, 1.34 GB) at 21:10:44, which led to high memory pressure (MemAvailable: 214MB) at 21:12:55. The memory pressure continued to increase, reaching a critical level (MemAvailable: 87MB) at 21:13:14. The kernel attempted to free up memory through compaction at 21:13:28, but ultimately had to kill the python3 process (pid 3847) at 21:13:41 due to the out-of-memory condition. This led to the termination of the inference worker service and a StatusCheck failure at 21:15:31.
llm_response.remediation: Increase the instance type to one with more memory, such as a c5.xlarge, to provide sufficient resources for the model loading and processing.
llm_response.aws_specific_notes: The current t3.large instance type has limited memory, which is not sufficient for the large model size. Switching to a fixed-performance instance type like c5.xlarge will provide more memory and better performance for the workload.

risks: Dependent services may be affected by the instance type change
risk_assessment.verification_steps: Verify that the new instance type has sufficient resources for the workload and that dependent services are not affected by the change.

nl_quality.name: nl_quality
nl_quality.details: The response accurately identifies the OOM root cause with precise log evidence and timestamps, provides a reasonable remediation, but the trust/risk section is generic rather than enumerating specific dependent services, and the c5.xlarge recommendation lacks justification for why compute-optimized over memory-optimized (e.g., r5) is appropriate for a memory-bound ML workload.
